scenario: Kestrel Bank models a Camunda 8.8 mortgage application process where a global event subprocess must react to multiple incoming reminders without terminating the main flow, handling 900 loans per day across 3 tenants in the same parent process model.
question: Which event subprocess configuration in Camunda 8.8 allows the subprocess to be triggered multiple times without terminating the containing scope?

options.text: A non-interrupting event subprocess in the same scope can be triggered multiple times during the lifetime of its containing scope without terminating active instances of that scope
An interrupting event subprocess can be re-triggered after each completion as long as no other event subprocess of the same scope has been triggered earlier in the same instance
A non-interrupting event subprocess that starts with an error start event can be triggered any number of times while the containing scope keeps running normally in the same instance
An interrupting event subprocess with a timer start event can fire multiple times per process instance, with the containing scope restored each time the subprocess completes its work

a.text: Correct. The event subprocesses page states that a non-interrupting event subprocess can be triggered multiple times while its containing scope remains active, contrasting with interrupting subprocesses which can be triggered only once.

b.text: Incorrect. An interrupting event subprocess can be triggered only once and terminates active instances of its containing scope, so it cannot be re-triggered later in the same parent process instance.

c.text: Incorrect. The page lists error as one allowed start event type but does not change interrupting semantics; only non-interrupting event subprocesses can fire multiple times in the same containing scope.

d.text: Incorrect. Interrupting event subprocesses cannot fire multiple times; the containing scope is terminated, not restored, when an interrupting event subprocess is triggered in the same instance.

explanation: Event subprocesses behave like boundary events but live inside the scope. Their start event is timer, message, error, signal, or escalation. Non-interrupting variants can be triggered multiple times while the containing scope stays active; interrupting variants run once and terminate that scope.
